sanskrit_devanagari:
त्रैगुण्यविषया वेदा निस्त्रैगुण्यो भवार्जुन ।
निर्द्वन्द्वो नित्यसत्त्वस्थो निर्योगक्षेम आत्मवान् ॥ ४५ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
trai-guṇya-viṣayā vedā nistrai-guṇyo bhavārjuna |
nirdvandvo nitya-sattva-stho niryoga-kṣema ātmavān || 45 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence byte-identical to canonical)
exact (text body identical; single danda terminator)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.45।।)
IAST exact (trai-guṇya-viṣayā vedā nistrai-guṇyo bhavārjuna nirdvandvo nitya-sattva-stho niryoga-kṣema ātmavān)

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.045). IAST body identical: 'traiguṇyaviṣayā vedā nistraiguṇyo bhavārjuna / nirdvaṃdvo nityasattvastho niryogakṣema ātmavān'. Anusvara-vs-assimilated-nasal orthographic variant on nirdvandva only.

anvaya.meaning: qualities-having (in compound; nistrai-guṇya = beyond the three)
purity / being / reality (in compound; commentators differ on guṇa-sense vs reality-sense)
established (in compound; nitya-sattva-stha = ever-poised in sattva)
preservation (in compound; niryoga-kṣema = free from acquiring-and-preserving anxiety)
self-possessed / firmly grounded in the self

translations.verbatim_quote: The Vedas deal mainly with the subject of the three modes of material nature. O Arjuna, become transcendental to these three modes. Be free from all dualities and from all anxieties for gain and safety, and be established in the self.

The Vedas deal with the three modes of material nature, O Arjun. Rise above the three modes to a state of pure spiritual consciousness. Freeing yourself from dualities, eternally fixed in Truth, and without concern for material gain and safety, be situated in the self.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: All material activities involve actions and reactions in the three modes of material nature. They are meant for fruitive results, which cause bondage in the material world. The Vedas deal mostly with fruitive activities to gradually elevate the general public from the field of sense gratification to a position on the transcendental plane.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.45 To those who are thus devoid of discriminating wisdom, who indulge in pleasure, O Arjuna, vedah, the Vedas; traigunya-visayah, have the three qualities as their object — i.e. the worldly life, as the object to be revealed. But you bhava, become; nistraigunyah, free from the three qualities, i.e. be free from desires.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads 'trai-guṇya-viṣayāḥ vedāḥ' carefully. The Vedas have the realm of the three guṇas as their object — i.e. the karma-kāṇḍa portion that addresses worldly life is what 2.45 is referring to. The injunction 'nistraigunyo bhava' (be free from the three guṇas) is therefore not a wholesale rejection of the Vedas; it is the call to rise above desire-bounded action. Shankara is explicit that 'nitya-sattva-stha' creates a tension — being 'ever-poised in sattva' while also being 'beyond the three (which include sattva)' looks contradictory. He resolves it by reading sattva here as 'reality/being' (the noun) or as 'pure-sattva preponderance functioning as a path-stage', not as the worldly guṇa called sattva. Niryoga-kṣema is read as freedom from grasping-after-acquisition (yoga) and clinging-to-preservation (kṣema). Ātmavān names the self-possessed practitioner.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.45 The word Traigunya means the three Gunas — Sattva, Rajas and Tamas. Here the term Traigunya denotes persons in whom Sattva, Rajas and Tamas are in abundance. The Vedas in prescribing desire-oriented rituals (Kamya-karmas) have such persons in view. Because of their great love, the Vedas teach what is good to those in whom Tamas, Rajas and Sattva preponderate.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja's reading is striking: 'trai-guṇya-viṣayāḥ vedāḥ' does NOT mean 'the Vedas themselves are bounded by the three guṇas.' It means the kāmya-karma portion of the Vedas has guṇa-dominated persons (those in whom sattva, rajas, tamas preponderate) as its addressees. The Vedas, out of compassion (great love), teach what is good to such persons at the level they can receive. The injunction 'nistraigunyo bhava' is therefore: rise above the guṇa-dominated state to which the kāmya-karma is addressed. Ramanuja is even more emphatic than Shankara that the verse is NOT anti-Vedic; he reframes the Vedas as compassionate pedagogy meeting students at their guṇa-state. 'Nitya-sattva-stha' is read as 'eternally established in pure sattva' as a stage of progress, with 'niryoga-kṣema' meaning the practitioner does not cling to acquiring or protecting outcomes. The end-state is ātmavān: self-possessed, firmly grounded.

positions.rendering: Vedas deal mainly with the three modes; become transcendental to them; be established in the self
Vedas deal with the three modes; rise above the three modes to pure spiritual consciousness; eternally fixed in Truth
Vedas have the three-guṇa-realm (worldly life) as their object [= karma-kāṇḍa scope]; be free from the three / be free from desires; sattva read as 'reality' to resolve apparent contradiction
trai-guṇya denotes persons in whom the guṇas preponderate; the kāmya-karma has such persons in view; rise above that addressed state; nitya-sattva-stha = eternally established in pure sattva as stage

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: This is the verse's classical interpretive crux. 'Trai-guṇya-viṣayāḥ vedāḥ' literally is 'the Vedas (whose) subject-matter (is) the three guṇas.' Shankara reads viṣaya as 'object/scope': the Vedas (specifically the karma-kāṇḍa) take the worldly-life-domain as their object. Ramanuja reads viṣaya as 'addressees': the Vedas teach the three-guṇa-bounded persons. Both readings preserve the Vedas' authority and scope the critique to the karma-kāṇḍa. Modern translations flatten to 'Vedas deal with the three modes', losing both nuances. The deeper crux is 'nitya-sattva-stha': how can one be both 'beyond the three guṇas' and 'ever-poised in sattva (which is one of the three)'? Shankara resolves by reading sattva here as 'reality/being' (the noun) or as a non-mixed, pure-sattva stage; Ramanuja reads it as a stage of progress in which sattva preponderates en route to nistrai-guṇya. The two are doctrinally similar; both prevent the translation from collapsing into self-contradiction. Niryoga-kṣema is unanimous: yoga = acquisition-of-the-not-yet-had, kṣema = preservation-of-the-already-had. The injunction is to be free of both anxious motions.

literal_meaning: The Vedas have the three guṇas as their subject-matter; rise beyond the three guṇas, O Arjuna. Be free from dualities, ever-poised in pure sattva / reality, free from acquisition-and-preservation concerns, self-possessed.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 2.45 closes the buddhi-yoga gateway begun at 2.41 by giving Arjuna the operative injunction. Three doctrinal moves. First: 'trai-guṇya-viṣayāḥ vedāḥ' scopes the Veda critique — the karma-kāṇḍa portion (Shankara: takes worldly-life as object; Ramanuja: addresses guṇa-preponderant persons) is what was criticised in 2.42-44. The Vedas as such are not voided. Second: 'nistrai-guṇyo bhava' is the imperative — rise above the three guṇa-dominated state. Third: qualifications of the one who has done so — nirdvandva (free from dualities), nitya-sattva-stha (ever-poised in pure sattva or in 'reality'; commentators preserve the apparent paradox), niryoga-kṣema (free from anxiety about acquisition and preservation), and ātmavān (self-possessed). Metaphysically loaded: 'transcend the three guṇas' is not 'have a good attitude' — it is a state-claim requiring careful engineering layering. The arc 2.42-45 is closed by 2.46 (small water-pond before the flood).

cross_references.explanation: 2.41 introduced vyavasāyātmikā buddhi as the resolute one-pointed intelligence; 2.45 specifies the state from which that buddhi must operate — nistrai-guṇya, nirdvandva, niryoga-kṣema, ātmavān.

2.42-43's critique of veda-vāda-rata is correctly scoped by 2.45's 'trai-guṇya-viṣayāḥ vedāḥ': it is the karma-kāṇḍa's three-guṇa-bounded addressees / object-domain that is being named, not the Vedas as such.

2.46 ('whatever utility a small water-pond has, that much is in all the Vedas for one who has realised') closes the same arc 2.42-45 opened. The two arcs together make 2.42-46 a coherent pedagogical block on Veda-scope.
The three guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) are taken up systematically in chapter 14. 2.45 is the first explicit injunction to rise beyond them; the metaphysical apparatus is elaborated only later.

remaining_gaps: Madhva's Brahma-Sampradaya commentary (which reportedly takes a different view on 'nitya-sattva-stha') not pulled.
